title: [Besu][EIP-7892] Chain split if BPO params at Osaka hardfork differ from Prague
description:
### Summary

In Besu, the `osakaDefinition` does not apply the blob schedule configured in the BPO config for the Osaka hardfork. Hence, during the Osaka hardfork, Besu will rely on the parameters defined in Prague.

https://github.com/hyperledger/besu/blob/82a3b50e83c834de2411444890bba98b0e26c603/ethereum/core/src/main/java/org/hyperledger/besu/ethereum/mainnet/MainnetProtocolSpecs.java#L935
```java
  static ProtocolSpecBuilder osakaDefinition(
      final Optional<BigInteger> chainId,
      final boolean enableRevertReason,
      final GenesisConfigOptions genesisConfigOptions,
      final EvmConfiguration evmConfiguration,
      final MiningConfiguration miningConfiguration,
      final boolean isParallelTxProcessingEnabled,
      final boolean isBlockAccessListEnabled,
      final MetricsSystem metricsSystem) {
    final long londonForkBlockNumber = genesisConfigOptions.getLondonBlockNumber().orElse(0L);

    return pragueDefinition(
            chainId,
            enableRevertReason,
            genesisConfigOptions,
            evmConfiguration,
            miningConfiguration,
            isParallelTxProcessingEnabled,
            isBlockAccessListEnabled,
            metricsSystem)
        .gasCalculator(OsakaGasCalculator::new)
        // tx gas limit cap EIP-7825
        .gasLimitCalculatorBuilder(
            (feeMarket, gasCalculator, blobSchedule) ->
                new OsakaTargetingGasLimitCalculator(
                    londonForkBlockNumber,
                    (BaseFeeMarket) feeMarket,
                    gasCalculator,
                    blobSchedule.getMax(),
                    blobSchedule.getTarget()))
        .evmBuilder(
            (gasCalculator, __) ->
                MainnetEVMs.osaka(gasCalculator, chainId.orElse(BigInteger.ZERO), evmConfiguration))
        .transactionValidatorFactoryBuilder(
            (evm, gasLimitCalculator, feeMarket) ->
                new TransactionValidatorFactory(
                    evm.getGasCalculator(),
                    gasLimitCalculator,
                    feeMarket,
                    true,
                    chainId,
                    Set.of(
                        TransactionType.FRONTIER,
                        TransactionType.ACCESS_LIST,
                        TransactionType.EIP1559,
                        TransactionType.BLOB,
                        TransactionType.DELEGATE_CODE),
                    Set.of(BlobType.KZG_CELL_PROOFS),
                    evm.getMaxInitcodeSize()))
        .transactionPoolPreProcessor(new OsakaTransactionPoolPreProcessor())
        .precompileContractRegistryBuilder(MainnetPrecompiledContractRegistries::osaka)
        .blockValidatorBuilder(MainnetBlockValidatorBuilder::osaka)
        .hardforkId(OSAKA);
  }
```

In case the Osaka hardfork in the BPO config were to use different blob parameters, a chain split will occur as Besu will use the incorrect blob parameters while other clients use the blob parameters defined in the BPO config for Osaka. 



### Root Cause

Does not call `applyBlobSchedule(builder, genesisConfigOptions, BlobScheduleOptions::getBpo1, OSAKA);` for `osakaDefinition`

### Internal Pre-conditions

Osaka blob parameters differ from Prague

### External Pre-conditions

-

### Attack Path

1. Osaka blob parameters differ from Prague
2. User sends blob transactions >max in Prague
3. Chain split as Besu rejects a block while other clients accept.

### Impact

more than 5% chain split.

### PoC

See the [gist](https://gist.github.com/3docSec/a568a71116291e99a1fd68a232badec3) for PoC.

### Mitigation

_No response_

title: Misleading assertNotNull parameter names for slashing processors in Eth2P2PNetworkBuilder
description:
### Summary

A mismatch between the asserted parameter name and the actual variable checked will cause a confusing/incorrect error message for operators and developers when the builder validation fails, as a null consumer variable will produce an error message that refers to a processor instead. This affects maintainability and debugging for Teku networking (teku/networking/eth2/src/main/java/tech/pegasys/teku/networking/eth2/Eth2P2PNetworkBuilder.java) as an operator or developer will be misled when diagnosing a missing configuration during network construction.

### Root Cause

In https://github.com/Consensys/teku/blob/master/networking/eth2/src/main/java/tech/pegasys/teku/networking/eth2/Eth2P2PNetworkBuilder.java#L601 and https://github.com/Consensys/teku/blob/master/networking/eth2/src/main/java/tech/pegasys/teku/networking/eth2/Eth2P2PNetworkBuilder.java#L603-L604 the validate() method uses assertNotNull calls where the parameter name string refers to a Processor but the actual variable checked is a Consumer:
```java
private void validate() {
  ...
  assertNotNull("gossipedAttestationProcessor", gossipedAttestationConsumer);
  ...
  assertNotNull("gossipedAttesterSlashingProcessor", gossipedAttesterSlashingConsumer); // <== 
  assertNotNull("gossipedProposerSlashingProcessor", gossipedProposerSlashingConsumer); // <== 
  ...
}
```
In other words: the message label passed to the assertion does not match the identifier being tested. This is most likely a copy/paste or naming inconsistency introduced during refactor.

### Internal Pre-conditions

1. A Teku build config/environment constructs Eth2P2PNetworkBuilder without setting one of the gossiped*Consumer fields (e.g., gossipedProposerSlashingConsumer is null).
2. The code path runs build() which calls validate() causing the assertNotNull check to fail.
3. The running developer/operator reads the assertion message from logs/exception.

### External Pre-conditions

None. This is a code-quality / maintainability issue that does not depend on external protocols.

### Attack Path

This is not an exploit; however, the practical reproduction steps that demonstrate the misleading behavior are:

1. A developer initializes Eth2P2PNetworkBuilder but omits setting gossipedProposerSlashingConsumer (or gossipedAttesterSlashingConsumer).
2. The developer calls build() on the builder.
3. validate() runs and throws an exception (or logs) with a message like:
```bash
gossipedProposerSlashingProcessor must not be null
```
even though the null variable is gossipedProposerSlashingConsumer.

### Impact

Developers/operators may be misled during debugging or production troubleshooting because the assertion message names a different component (Processor) than the real missing field (Consumer). This increases developer time-to-fix and risks misdiagnosis during incident response. It does not directly affect consensus, security, or runtime behavior beyond causing confusing validation failures.

### PoC

none.

### Mitigation

Recommended fixes (pick one of the two styles depending on preferred naming convention):
1. Replace the two lines with:
```java
	...
	assertNotNull("gossipedAttestationProcessor", gossipedAttestationProcessor);
	...
	assertNotNull("gossipedAttesterSlashingProcessor", gossipedAttesterSlashingProcessor);  
    	assertNotNull("gossipedProposerSlashingProcessor", gossipedProposerSlashingProcessor); 
	...
```
Rename the fields gossipedAttesterSlashingConsumer → gossipedAttesterSlashingProcessor and gossipedProposerSlashingConsumer → gossipedProposerSlashingProcessor across the builder and all places they are used (larger refactor).

Suggested minimal patch (diff-style):
```diff
--- a/networking/eth2/src/main/java/tech/pegasys/teku/networking/eth2/Eth2P2PNetworkBuilder.java
+++ b/networking/eth2/src/main/java/tech/pegasys/teku/networking/eth2/Eth2P2PNetworkBuilder.java
@@ -598,8 +598,8 @@
     assertNotNull("gossipedAttestationProcessor", gossipedAttestationConsumer);
     ...
-    assertNotNull("gossipedAttesterSlashingProcessor", gossipedAttesterSlashingConsumer);
-    assertNotNull("gossipedProposerSlashingProcessor", gossipedProposerSlashingConsumer); 
+    assertNotNull("gossipedAttesterSlashingConsumer", gossipedAttesterSlashingConsumer);
+    assertNotNull("gossipedProposerSlashingConsumer", gossipedProposerSlashingConsumer);
     assertNotNull("gossipedVoluntaryExitProcessor", gossipedVoluntaryExitConsumer);
```

title: EstimateGas Not Enforce MaxTxnGaslimit By EIP-7825 in Erigon
description:
### Summary

**`EIP-7825`** mandates a cap on transaction gas limits, which is properly enforced in the state and transaction pool. However, this enforcement is missing in EstimateGas. This oversight can lead to an overflowed gas assignment for transactions and a lack of user feedback when interacting with the `eth_estimateGas` API on an Erigon node.

### Root Cause

Code Commit: 
https://github.com/erigontech/erigon/tree/2224c7afe3b5179fafb2ec659e0e789259ac2f54
 
In the method `EstimateGas` of `APIImpl`, a high cap is assigned to the block gas limit as code snippet shown below: 
https://github.com/erigontech/erigon/blob/main/rpc/jsonrpc/eth_call.go#L207
```go=207
// EstimateGas implements eth_estimateGas. Returns an estimate of how much gas is necessary to allow the transaction to complete. The transaction will not be added to the blockchain.
func (api *APIImpl) EstimateGas(ctx context.Context, argsOrNil *ethapi2.CallArgs, blockNrOrHash *rpc.BlockNumberOrHash, overrides *ethapi2.StateOverrides) (hexutil.Uint64, error) {

	// Retrieve the block to act as the gas ceiling
		hi = header.GasLimit
	// Recap the highest gas allowance with specified gascap.
	if hi > api.GasCap {
		log.Warn("Caller gas above allowance, capping", "requested", hi, "cap", api.GasCap)
		hi = api.GasCap
	}
	}

```
When using the `estimateGas` API for transaction simulation, the returned gas limit might not be appropriate for determining the actual gas limit required before sending a transaction to the mempool. This is because the upper bound of the gas limit is set to the block's gas limit, or the APIGasCap, which can exceed the maxTxGas.

Reference: 
- https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-7825.md


### Internal Pre-conditions

The absence of an internal precondition stems from the function's failure to adhere to **`EIP-7825`**, which caps single transaction gas limit estimations.

### External Pre-conditions

Users can utilize the `eth_estimateGas` API to determine the optimal gas limit for a transaction before it is published on-chain.
```shell
curl -s -X POST localhost:8545 -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  --data '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"eth_estimateGas","params":[{"from":"0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000001","gas":"0xFFFFFFFFFFFF"}]}'
```


### Attack Path

1. Users utilize the eth_estimateGas API with a very large gas cap from the API input, just to make sure that the transaction will not be failed due to lower gas consumption
2. A very large gas estimated larger than the gas cap `16,777,216` in EIP-7825
3. Users send the transaction with this large gas and get rejected when validating before adding into the mempool
4. This make the UX not very well from the beginning when interacting with the chain

### Impact

To defend against invalid on-chain transactions, both new transaction validation in the mempool and state transition transaction validation enforcement have been implemented. However, EIP-7825's backward compatibility is poor, which will negatively impact user experience (UX), especially for transactions requiring significant gas consumption. Therefore, the RPC should provide hints and block similar actions to improve overall UX.
In short, the impact is low but it could be helpful to improve the UX by enforcing the gas cap anyway. Refer to Geth [`PR-32348`](https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/pull/32348).


### PoC

_No response_

### Mitigation

_No response_

title: [Nimbus][EIP-7594] Nimbus accepts duplicate sidecars when proposer publishes multiple blocks
description:
### Summary

Clients are consistently following the spec of [ignoring duplicate sidecars based on the (block_header.slot, block_header.proposer_index, sidecar.index) tuple](https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/blob/master/specs/fulu/p2p-interface.md#data_column_sidecar_subnet_id).

Nimbus however [also includes the block_root in the key](https://github.com/status-im/nimbus-eth2/blob/660ebeee3aa25c236b5c1fd358dd8a8d3b50d32e/beacon_chain/gossip_processing/gossip_validation.nim#L621), which means that if a proposer publishes two different blocks for the same slot, Nimbus will accept and forward sidecars for both.

```nim
  if dataColumnQuarantine[].hasSidecar(
      block_root, block_header.slot, block_header.proposer_index, data_column_sidecar.index):
    return errIgnore("DataColumnSidecar: already have valid data column from same proposer")
```

This deviates from the spec, but the only impact is increased CPU utilization and requires intentional equivocation, but it is still good to be fixed to follow the spec.

title: [Teku][EIP-7594] RPC Handler crash `DataColumnSidecarsByRange` through ArithmeticException
Uninitialized channel in AbstractGossipManager may cause unexpected "Gossip channel not available" failures
Hanging SafeFuture returned after shutdown in ScheduledExecutorAsyncRunner
Lost sync committee messages when state or assignments are unavailable in SyncCommitteeMessageGossipManager
description:
### Summary

Failing to compute or retrieve the applicable subcommittee assignments will cause a message publication failure / lost sync committee message for validators and node operators as the manager will log an error and drop the message (no retry, no queuing), meaning locally-produced sync committee messages may never be gossiped to the network.

Affected file: teku/networking/eth2/src/main/java/tech/pegasys/teku/networking/eth2/gossip/SyncCommitteeMessageGossipManager.java

### Root Cause

In https://github.com/Consensys/teku/blob/master/networking/eth2/src/main/java/tech/pegasys/teku/networking/eth2/gossip/SyncCommitteeMessageGossipManager.java#L74-L88 the publish(ValidatableSyncCommitteeMessage) path that computes subcommittee assignments will, when assignments are not present, call syncCommitteeStateUtils.getStateForSyncCommittee(...) and on failure simply log an error and drop the message:
```java
syncCommitteeStateUtils
    .getStateForSyncCommittee(message.getSlot())
    .finish(
        maybeState ->
            maybeState.ifPresentOrElse(
                state ->
                    publish(
                        message,
                        message
                            .calculateAssignments(spec, state)
                            .getAssignedSubcommittees()),
                () ->
                    LOG.error(
                        "Failed to publish sync committee message for slot {} because the applicable subnets could not be calculated",
                        message.getSlot())),
        error ->
            LOG.error(
                "Failed to retrieve state for sync committee message at slot {}",
                message.getSlot(),
                error));
```
When getStateForSyncCommittee returns empty or fails, the code logs an error and does not retry, buffer, or notify the caller resulting in permanent loss of the message.

### Internal Pre-conditions

1. {Validator/node component} produces a ValidatableSyncCommitteeMessage without a receivedSubnetId and without embedded subcommitteeAssignments (i.e., message.getSubcommitteeAssignments() is empty).
2. SyncCommitteeMessageGossipManager.publish(message) is invoked and syncCommitteeStateUtils.getStateForSyncCommittee(message.getSlot()) either:
- returns Optional.empty() (state not available yet), or
- completes exceptionally (I/O / DB error / race).
3. No higher layer re-submits the message and the manager does not queue the message for retry.

### External Pre-conditions

None required. This is an internal handling/design issue and does not depend on external protocol state changes. (It may be aggravated by external conditions such as heavy reorgs or slow state availability, but those are environmental, not protocol preconditions.)

### Attack Path

1. A validator/node component generates a local ValidatableSyncCommitteeMessage at slot S without receivedSubnetId (local message).
2. publish(message) is called. message.getSubcommitteeAssignments() is empty.
3. syncCommitteeStateUtils.getStateForSyncCommittee(S) is invoked but:
- the node is temporarily missing the required beacon state for slot S (reorg or sync lag), or
- the state retrieval fails due to transient error.
4. The completion handler logs an error and the code path returns without publishing or scheduling a retry. The message is dropped.


### Impact

1. Local sync committee messages may never be gossiped, reducing the validator’s participation in sync committee duties.
2. This can lead to missed duties or decreased effectiveness of the sync committee, potentially reducing earned rewards for the validator and degrading the node’s contribution to network liveness.
3. At scale (many nodes or many dropped messages), this can reduce overall sync committee coverage and harm network performance.

### PoC

SyncCommiteeMessageGossipManagerTest.java:
```java
@Test
  void shouldFailToPublishWhenStateRetrievalFails() {
    final ValidatableSyncCommitteeMessage message =
        ValidatableSyncCommitteeMessage.fromValidator(
            dataStructureUtil.randomSyncCommitteeMessage());

    // Simulate state retrieval failure
    final RuntimeException exception = new RuntimeException("State not available");
    when(syncCommitteeStateUtils.getStateForSyncCommittee(message.getSlot()))
        .thenReturn(SafeFuture.failedFuture(exception));

    gossipManager.publish(message);

    // Verify that no gossip is taking place
    verify(subnetSubscriptions, never()).gossip(any(), anyInt());

    // Here we can only rely on error logs,
    // because publish() does not expose errors to the caller and does not retry.
    // For PoC, we make sure publishFailureCounter increases.
    assertThat(publishFailureCounter.getValue()).isEqualTo(1);
  }
```

### Mitigation

1. Buffer & retry: If getStateForSyncCommittee returns empty or fails, buffer the message and retry when the required state becomes available (subscribe to a state-ready event or poll a bounded number of times with backoff).
2. Return feedback to caller: Change publish(ValidatableSyncCommitteeMessage) to return a SafeFuture<Void> or similar so callers can detect success/failure and decide to retry.
3. Retry on transient failures: On transient errors (exceptions), attempt limited retries with exponential backoff before giving up. Increment metrics for retries to surface problematic conditions.

Example minimal patch (queue + single retry-on-state-availability):
```diff
--- a/.../SyncCommitteeMessageGossipManager.java
+++ b/.../SyncCommitteeMessageGossipManager.java
@@
-      } else {
-        syncCommitteeStateUtils
-            .getStateForSyncCommittee(message.getSlot())
-            .finish(
-                maybeState ->
-                    maybeState.ifPresentOrElse(
-                        state ->
-                            publish(
-                                message,
-                                message
-                                    .calculateAssignments(spec, state)
-                                    .getAssignedSubcommittees()),
-                        () ->
-                            LOG.error(
-                                "Failed to publish sync committee message for slot {} because the applicable subnets could not be calculated",
-                                message.getSlot())),
-                error ->
-                    LOG.error(
-                        "Failed to retrieve state for sync committee message at slot {}",
-                        message.getSlot(),
-                        error));
-      }
+      } else {
+        // Try to obtain state and publish. If state is not available, buffer and retry once when
+        // state becomes available to avoid dropping locally produced messages.
+        syncCommitteeStateUtils
+            .getStateForSyncCommittee(message.getSlot())
+            .finish(
+                maybeState -> {
+                  if (maybeState.isPresent()) {
+                    final UInt64 slot = message.getSlot();
+                    final Set<Integer> assigned =
+                        message.calculateAssignments(spec, maybeState.get()).getAssignedSubcommittees();
+                    publish(message, assigned);
+                    return;
+                  }
+                  // buffer message for a short period and register a one-time callback to retry
+                  bufferAndRetryWhenStateAvailable(message, /*maxRetries=*/1, Duration.ofMillis(200));
+                },
+                error -> {
+                  LOG.warn(
+                      "Error retrieving state for sync committee message at slot {} - will retry once",
+                      message.getSlot(),
+                      error);
+                  bufferAndRetryWhenStateAvailable(message, /*maxRetries=*/1, Duration.ofMillis(200));
+                });
+      }

```

Add helper skeleton:
```java
private final LoadingCache<UInt64, List<ValidatableSyncCommitteeMessage>> retryBuffer =
    CacheBuilder.newBuilder()
        .expireAfterWrite(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
        .build();

private void bufferAndRetryWhenStateAvailable(
    final ValidatableSyncCommitteeMessage message, final int maxRetries, final Duration delay) {
  // Store in buffer
  retryBuffer.asMap().computeIfAbsent(message.getSlot(), k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(message);
  // Schedule short delayed retry (or hook into state-ready notifications)
  // Pseudo-code: async scheduler: after delay -> attempt getStateForSyncCommittee and publish buffered messages
}
```

title: Nimbus: queryRandom lacks subnet overlap checks
description:
### Summary

The `queryRandom` function in Nimbus PeerDAS implementation is used to discover peers for data column distribution.

This function compares custody group counts instead of checking actual subnet overlap. 
The breaks PeerDAS peer discovery mechanism, as incorrect peers will be selected.

### Root Cause

Let's look at the code:

```nim

proc queryRandom*(
    d: Eth2DiscoveryProtocol,
    forkId: ENRForkID,
    wantedAttnets: AttnetBits,
    wantedSyncnets: SyncnetBits,
    wantedCgcnets: CgcBits,
    minScore: int): Future[seq[Node]] {.async: (raises: [CancelledError]).} =
  ## Perform a discovery query for a random target
  ## (forkId) and matching at least one of the attestation subnets.

  let nodes = await d.queryRandom()

  var filtered: seq[(int, Node)]
  for n in nodes:
    var score: int = 0

    let
      eth2FieldBytes = n.record.get(enrForkIdField, seq[byte]).valueOr:
        continue
      peerForkId =
        try:
          SSZ.decode(eth2FieldBytes, ENRForkID)
        except SerializationError as e:
          debug "Could not decode the eth2 field of peer",
            peer = n.record.toURI(), exception = e.name, msg = e.msg
          continue

    if not forkId.isCompatibleForkId(peerForkId):
      continue

    let cgcCountBytes = n.record.get(enrCustodySubnetCountField, seq[byte])
    if cgcCountBytes.isOk():
      let cgcCountNode =
        try:
          SSZ.decode(cgcCountBytes.get(), uint8)
        except SerializationError as e:
          debug "Could not decode the cgc ENR field of peer",
            peer = n.record.toURI(), exception = e.name, msg = e.msg
          continue

 1)     if wantedCgcnets.countOnes().uint8 == cgcCountNode:
        score += 1

    let attnetsBytes = n.record.get(enrAttestationSubnetsField, seq[byte])
    if attnetsBytes.isOk():
      let attnetsNode =
        try:
          SSZ.decode(attnetsBytes.get(), AttnetBits)
        except SerializationError as e:
          debug "Could not decode the attnets ENR bitfield of peer",
            peer = n.record.toURI(), exception = e.name, msg = e.msg
          continue

      for i in 0..<ATTESTATION_SUBNET_COUNT:
        if wantedAttnets[i] and attnetsNode[i]:
          score += 1

    let syncnetsBytes = n.record.get(enrSyncSubnetsField, seq[byte])
    if syncnetsBytes.isOk():
      let syncnetsNode =
        try:
          SSZ.decode(syncnetsBytes.get(), SyncnetBits)
        except SerializationError as e:
          debug "Could not decode the syncnets ENR bitfield of peer",
            peer = n.record.toURI(), exception = e.name, msg = e.msg
          continue

      for i in SyncSubcommitteeIndex:
        if wantedSyncnets[i] and syncnetsNode[i]:
          score += 10 # connecting to the right syncnet is urgent

    if score >= minScore:
      filtered.add((score, n))

  d.rng[].shuffle(filtered)
  return filtered.sortedByIt(-it[0]).mapIt(it[1])

```

### Notes

1) On this line code just compares number of ones with CGC count, there are no column overlap checks.


Main user of `queryRandom`  function is this function, which runs every 5 seconds:

```
proc runDiscoveryLoop(node: Eth2Node) {.async: (raises: [CancelledError]).} =
  debug "Starting discovery loop"

  while true:
    let
      currentEpoch = node.getBeaconTime().slotOrZero.epoch
      (wantedAttnets, wantedSyncnets, wantedCgcnets) = node.getLowSubnets(currentEpoch)
      wantedAttnetsCount = wantedAttnets.countOnes()
      wantedSyncnetsCount = wantedSyncnets.countOnes()
      wantedCgcnetsCount = wantedCgcnets.countOnes()
      outgoingPeers = node.peerPool.lenCurrent({PeerType.Outgoing})
      targetOutgoingPeers = max(node.wantedPeers div 10, 3)

    if wantedAttnetsCount > 0 or wantedSyncnetsCount > 0 or
        wantedCgcnetsCount > 0 or outgoingPeers < targetOutgoingPeers:

      let
        minScore =
          if wantedAttnetsCount > 0 or wantedSyncnetsCount > 0 or
              wantedCgcnetsCount > 0:
            1
          else:
            0
        discoveredNodes = await node.discovery.queryRandom(
          node.discoveryForkId,
          wantedAttnets,
          wantedSyncnets,
          wantedCgcnets,
          minScore)

    ...

    await sleepAsync(5.seconds)
```

### Internal Pre-conditions

Fulu fork has to be activated.

### External Pre-conditions

None

### Attack Path

The issue does not require an active attacker. It could happen during normal node operations.

### Impact

Node will connect to peers based on matching CGC counts rather than subnet overlap. 

As a result node will not be able to retrieve required data columns.

### PoC

Proof of concept is quite simple, it demonstrates that queryRandom selects peers based only on cgc.

1) add the following test to tests/test_discovery.nim
```nim

  asyncTest "queryRandom issue":
    let
      node1 = generateNode(rng, Port(5000))
      # Node 2, custody group count = 3
      node2 = generateNode(rng, Port(5001), {
        enrForkIdField: SSZ.encode(enrForkId),
        enrCustodySubnetCountField: SSZ.encode(3'u8)
      })
      # Node 3, custody group count = 2  
      node3 = generateNode(rng, Port(5002), {
        enrForkIdField: SSZ.encode(enrForkId),
        enrCustodySubnetCountField: SSZ.encode(2'u8)
      })

    node1.open()
    node2.open()
    node3.open()

    check (await node2.ping(node1.localNode)).isOk()
    check (await node3.ping(node1.localNode)).isOk()

    var wantedCgcnets: CgcBits
    wantedCgcnets.setBit(5)
    wantedCgcnets.setBit(10) 
    wantedCgcnets.setBit(15)

    let discovered = await node1.queryRandom(
      enrForkId, AttnetBits(), noSyncnetsPreference,
      wantedCgcnets, 1)

    echo "XXXXKE discovered ", discovered.len
    for node in discovered:
      if node.record.toURI() == node2.localNode.record.toURI():
        echo "Node 2 (custody_count=3) was selected"
      elif node.record.toURI() == node3.localNode.record.toURI():
        echo "Node 3 (custody_count=2) was selected"
      else:
        echo "Unknown node: ", node.record.toURI()
 
    check discovered.len == 1
    
    await node1.closeWait()
    await node2.closeWait()  
    await node3.closeWait()

```

2) run it:
```
$ ./env.sh bash
$ nim c -r tests/test_discovery.nim
...
XXXXKE discovered 1
Node 2 (custody_count=3) was selected

```

### Mitigation

_No response_

title: Proposer calculation can be incorrect since proposer lookahead is not considered inside get_beacon_proposer_indices
Mismatch Between Osaka Engine API Spec And Erigon Implementation May Lead to CL Handling Issues
description:
### Summary

The Erigon implementation deviates significantly from the Osaka Engine API specification, specifically impacting the `engine_getBlobsV2` method. Strict adherence to the specification is crucial, as any discrepancies in the interaction between the Execution Layer (EL) and Consensus Layer (CL) could lead to disastrous inconsistencies.


### Root Cause

Code Repository: https://github.com/erigontech/erigon at main branch
Code Commit: https://github.com/erigontech/erigon/tree/38e6c19af042de2f9b819b19ccc173339e79bc1a

The implementation is mainly in the file of `engine_server.go` where the functions serving for the corresponding engine APIs. 
https://github.com/erigontech/erigon/blob/38e6c19af042de2f9b819b19ccc173339e79bc1a/execution/engineapi/engine_server.go#L1026
```go
func (e *EngineServer) getBlobs(ctx context.Context, blobHashes []common.Hash, version clparams.StateVersion) (any, error) {

 } else if version == clparams.CapellaVersion {
    ret := make([]*engine_types.BlobAndProofV1, len(blobHashes))


```
The BlobAndProofV1 was introduced in Deneb/Cancun, not Capella/Shanghai, as per the specification:[ https://github.com/ethereum/execution-apis/blob/main/src/engine/cancun.md](https://github.com/ethereum/execution-apis/blob/main/src/engine/cancun.md).

Therefore, the version mentioned should be DenebVersion. Using the incorrect version will result in a wrong response and potentially lead to severe inconsistencies, particularly when handling the engine_getBlobsV1 request.

Besides, according to the [`Osaka Engine API Spec`](https://github.com/ethereum/execution-apis/blob/main/src/engine/osaka.md), as quoted below: 
```text
For the following methods:
[engine_getBlobsV1](https://github.com/ethereum/execution-apis/blob/main/src/engine/cancun.md#engine_getblobsv1)
a validation MUST be added:
Client software MUST return -38005: Unsupported fork error if the Osaka fork has been activated.
```
The Osaka/Fulu fork check is missing, which should be implemented like this before entering the switch branch. 
```go
(!s.config.IsOsaka(ts) && version >= clparams.FuluVersion) ||
    (s.config.IsOsaka(ts) && version < clparams.FuluVersion) {
    return nil, &rpc.UnsupportedForkError{Message: "Unsupported fork"}
```

Reference: 
[`Osaka Engine API Spec`](https://github.com/ethereum/execution-apis/blob/main/src/engine/osaka.md)

### Internal Pre-conditions

The wrong branch to an incorrect fork version in the aforementioned code snippet.

### External Pre-conditions

Interactions with CL clients using the `engine_getBlobsV1` method, even with the correct Deneb fork version, do not return the expected response as mandated by the Engine API.


### Attack Path

1. During the Fusaka upgrade, the Erigon node will connect to the CL nodes during the upgrading phase: clients with version e.g. `Deneb`
2. The legacy `engine_getBlobsV1` method is still being utilized to fetch blobs from the execution layer blob pool;
3. Due to wrong branch implementation, the blobs could not be returned by the Erigon EL node from the blob mempool
4. This will lead to CL node missing the blobs to continue the beacon block processing


### Impact

The `engine_getBlobs` API plays a critical role in the seamless operation of the Ethereum network, specifically bridging the Consensus Layer (CL) and the Execution Layer (EL). Its primary function is to enable the CL, responsible for maintaining the blockchain's state and validating blocks, to retrieve essential data known as "blobs" from the EL client's blob pool. These blobs, which contain transaction data and other relevant information, are absolutely necessary for the CL to successfully process beacon blocks.
Without the timely and accurate retrieval of these blobs via the `engine_getBlobs` API, several severe issues can arise. Foremost among them are block processing errors. If the CL cannot access the required blob data, it will be unable to properly validate and include transactions within a block. This can lead to a state of inconsistency between the two layers, hindering the network's ability to reach consensus.
Furthermore, a failure in this process can result in the creation of "empty blocks." An empty block is one that is proposed by a validator but contains no transactions or an incomplete set of transactions due to the absence of necessary blob data. This outcome is highly inefficient and detrimental to the network, as it leads to a "wasting of valuable slots." Each slot represents a fixed time interval during which a new block is expected to be proposed and finalized. If a slot is used to publish an empty or invalid block, the network's throughput is reduced, and the overall progress of the blockchain is slowed down.
The implications of these issues are significant. Block processing errors can lead to network instability, forks, and a degradation of user experience due to delayed transaction finality. Empty blocks, while not immediately catastrophic, represent a lost opportunity for processing transactions and contribute to network congestion. Both scenarios undermine the reliability and efficiency of the Ethereum network. Therefore, the robust and uninterrupted functionality of the `engine_getBlobs` API is paramount, and any disruptions to its operation constitute a serious issue demanding immediate attention and mitigation strategies to ensure the continued health and performance of the blockchain.

### PoC

_No response_

### Mitigation

_No response_

title: [INFO] besu-native implementation of `p256_verify` unnecessarily checks twice that the public key is on the secp256r1 curve
description:
### Summary

In [p256_verify](https://github.com/hyperledger/besu-native/blob/main/boringssl/boringssl_jni/p256_verify.c#L94-L96) from the besu-native repository, used by the besu client as a native implementation for the `P256VERIFY` precompile as part of EIP-7951, the `EC_POINT_is_on_curve` is used as an additional check to confirm that the public key is on the curve `NID_X9_62_prime256v1`. 

However, this check is unnecessary, since `EC_POINT_oct2point` already performs this validation, as explained on [Boringssl's documentation](https://commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-boringssl-docs/ec.h.html#EC_POINT_oct2point), and confirmed on its implementation (the underlying [ec_GFp_simple_oct2point](https://github.com/google/boringssl/blob/dcaedb5676a70ed5dd5f6b365bf3dbef4bf79d88/crypto/fipsmodule/ec/oct.cc.inc#L151) uses [EC_POINT_set_compressed_coordinates_GFp](https://github.com/google/boringssl/blob/dcaedb5676a70ed5dd5f6b365bf3dbef4bf79d88/crypto/fipsmodule/ec/oct.cc.inc#L138) checks for [EC_POINT_set_affine_coordinates_GFp](https://github.com/google/boringssl/blob/dcaedb5676a70ed5dd5f6b365bf3dbef4bf79d88/crypto/fipsmodule/ec/oct.cc.inc#L288) return value, which uses [ec_point_set_affine_coordinates](https://github.com/google/boringssl/blob/dcaedb5676a70ed5dd5f6b365bf3dbef4bf79d88/crypto/fipsmodule/ec/ec.cc.inc#L601), which does the [actual curve check](https://github.com/google/boringssl/blob/dcaedb5676a70ed5dd5f6b365bf3dbef4bf79d88/crypto/fipsmodule/ec/ec.cc.inc#L559-L577))

### Root Cause

Unnecessary `EC_POINT_is_on_curve` since this is already checked internally in `EC_POINT_oct2point`

### Internal Pre-conditions

No preconditions

### External Pre-conditions

No preconditions

### Attack Path

No attack. Code optimization.

### Impact

Unnecessary computation on Besu nodes

### PoC

See code flow

### Mitigation

Remove the unnecessary `EC_POINT_is_on_curve` duplicate check

title: [INFO] Unreachable code path in besu-native's implementation of `p256_verify` as part of EIP-7951
description:
### Summary

In besu-native's [p256_verify](https://github.com/hyperledger/besu-native/blob/main/boringssl/boringssl_jni/p256_verify.c#L125-L127), used by the Besu client as a native implementation of the `P256VERIFY` precompile from EIP-7951, it checks if the return value from `ECDSA_do_verify` is different than 0 or 1, and creates a different error message for failure.

```C
  } else {
    snprintf(error_message_buf, error_message_buf_len, "%s", "internal error during signature verification");
    ret = 2;
  } 
```

However, this is impossible, as highlighted on [Boringssl's documentation](https://commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-boringssl-docs/ecdsa.h.html#ECDSA_verify), and confirmed on [its implementation](https://github.com/google/boringssl/blob/dcaedb5676a70ed5dd5f6b365bf3dbef4bf79d88/crypto/ecdsa/ecdsa_asn1.cc#L107-L137). The return value of `ECDSA_verify` is always a boolean represented by either 0 or 1. As a result, `ret = 2` can never happen, and this is dead code.

### Root Cause

Unreachable code path in `p256_verify`

### Internal Pre-conditions

No preconditions

### External Pre-conditions

No preconditions

### Attack Path

No attack. Code optimization.

### Impact

Larger code size than necessary.

### PoC

See above.

### Mitigation

Remove the code path that checks if `verify_status` is different than 0 or 1

title: Malicious gossip peer will stall PeerDAS sampling for custody validators
description:
### Summary

Marking PeerDAS sidecars as seen before verification will cause data availability loss for custody validators as a malicious gossip peer will replay invalid sidecars to poison the cache (High severity).


### Root Cause

In https://github.com/erigontech/erigon/blob/73b6886a812cce6403121068511f401827ac54b3/cl/phase1/network/services/data_column_sidecar_service.go#L70-L118 the service stores each `(slot, proposer, index)` tuple in `seenSidecar` before running any proof or signature checks, so a failing verification leaves the entry cached and blocks the valid retry for the same tuple.

### Internal Pre-conditions

1. A custody validator is running PeerDAS sampling and accepts gossip from the attacker-controlled peer on the relevant column subnet.
2. The attacker delivers an invalid `data_column_sidecar` for a target `(slot, proposer_index, index)` tuple before any honest peer relays the valid sidecar.

### External Pre-conditions

1. None.


### Attack Path

1. Malicious peer crafts or forwards a `data_column_sidecar` with a correct header tuple but bogus column data/KZG proof.
2. The peer gossips this payload to the target validator first; `ProcessMessage` caches the tuple (`seenSidecar.Add`) and then throws `invalid data column sidecar` during verification.
3. When the honest sidecar for the same tuple arrives, the validator immediately hits `ErrIgnore` because the tuple is already marked as seen, so the custody column stays unavailable.
4. Repeating the poisoning for each custody column keeps the group below `SAMPLES_PER_SLOT`, triggering recoveries and ultimately preventing the network from finalizing those slots.

### Impact

The consensus layer loses PeerDAS availability for the affected slots; spreading the poisoning across enough custody validators can block >33% of the network from finalizing, giving the attacker a low-cost, high-impact denial-of-service lever without slashing exposure.


### PoC

**Unit test (`cl/phase1/network/services/poc_fulu_peer_das_sampling_test.go`)** 
```go
package services

import (
    "context"
    "testing"

    "github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
    "go.uber.org/mock/gomock"

    "github.com/erigontech/erigon/cl/beacon/beaconevents"
    syncedmock "github.com/erigontech/erigon/cl/beacon/synced_data/mock_services"
    "github.com/erigontech/erigon/cl/clparams"
    "github.com/erigontech/erigon/cl/cltypes"
    blobmock "github.com/erigontech/erigon/cl/persistence/blob_storage/mock_services"
    forkchoicemock "github.com/erigontech/erigon/cl/phase1/forkchoice/mock_services"
    "github.com/erigontech/erigon/cl/utils/eth_clock"
)

func TestPocFuluPeerDasSampling_InvalidSidecarPoisonsCache(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    ctrl := gomock.NewController(t)
    defer ctrl.Finish()

    forkChoice := forkchoicemock.NewForkChoiceStorageMock(t)
    forkChoice.MockPeerDas.EXPECT().IsArchivedMode().Return(true).AnyTimes()
    forkChoice.MockPeerDas.EXPECT().IsColumnOverHalf(gomock.Any(), gomock.Any()).Return(false).AnyTimes()
    forkChoice.MockPeerDas.EXPECT().IsBlobAlreadyRecovered(gomock.Any()).Return(false).AnyTimes()

    forkChoice.Headers[testParentRoot] = &cltypes.BeaconBlockHeader{Slot: testSlot - 1}

    syncedData := syncedmock.NewMockSyncedData(ctrl)
    syncedData.EXPECT().Syncing().Return(false).Times(2)
    syncedData.EXPECT().ViewHeadState(gomock.Any()).Times(0)

    clock := eth_clock.NewMockEthereumClock(ctrl)
    clock.EXPECT().GetCurrentSlot().Return(testSlot).AnyTimes()

    storage := blobmock.NewMockDataColumnStorage(ctrl)
    storage.EXPECT().WriteColumnSidecars(gomock.Any(), gomock.Any(), gomock.Any(), gomock.Any()).Times(0)

    service := NewDataColumnSidecarService(
        &clparams.BeaconChainConfig{
            SlotsPerEpoch:   testSlotsPerEpoch,
            NumberOfColumns: 4,
        },
        clock,
        forkChoice,
        syncedData,
        storage,
        beaconevents.NewEventEmitter(),
    )

    originalVerifyData := verifyDataColumnSidecar
    originalVerifyInclusion := verifyDataColumnSidecarInclusionProof
    originalVerifyKZG := verifyDataColumnSidecarKZGProofs
    originalBlsVerify := blsVerify
    defer func() {
        verifyDataColumnSidecar = originalVerifyData
        verifyDataColumnSidecarInclusionProof = originalVerifyInclusion
        verifyDataColumnSidecarKZGProofs = originalVerifyKZG
        blsVerify = originalBlsVerify
    }()

    callCount := 0
    verifyDataColumnSidecar = func(sidecar *cltypes.DataColumnSidecar) bool {
        callCount++
        return callCount > 1
    }
    verifyDataColumnSidecarInclusionProof = func(*cltypes.DataColumnSidecar) bool { return true }
    verifyDataColumnSidecarKZGProofs = func(*cltypes.DataColumnSidecar) bool { return true }
    blsVerify = func(signature []byte, msg []byte, publicKey []byte) (bool, error) {
        return true, nil
    }

    sidecar := createMockDataColumnSidecar(testSlot, 0)

    err := service.ProcessMessage(context.Background(), nil, sidecar)
    require.Error(t, err)
    require.Contains(t, err.Error(), "invalid data column sidecar")

    err = service.ProcessMessage(context.Background(), nil, sidecar)
    require.ErrorIs(t, err, ErrIgnore, "FULU-PEERDAS-SAMPLING should cause cached tuple to ignore valid retry")
}
```
_Run command:_
```bash
go test ./cl/phase1/network/services -run TestPocFuluPeerDasSampling_InvalidSidecarPoisonsCache -count=1
```
(The project currently has no integration PoC; this unit test demonstrates the cache poisoning end-to-end.)

### Mitigation

Move `seenSidecar.Add` to execute only after all `verifyDataColumnSidecar*` checks and proposer signature verification succeed, or delete the cache entry on error so only validated sidecars populate the lookahead window.
rejection_comment: Erigon CL is out of scope, only the EL side was in scope.

title: Concurrent pre-check race can cause duplicate work
ENR custody group count spoofing drives sampling failure and selective eclipse
Goroutine Fan-out under Burst Load in P2P Broadcaster enables Local DoS
description:
### Summary

 An adversarial peer or adverse network conditions will cause CPU and memory exhaustion for beacon node operators
https://github.com/sherlock-audit/2025-09-ethereum-fusaka-upgrade/blob/main/scope/prysm/beacon-chain/p2p/broadcaster.go

A missing concurrency limit and long per-broadcast timeouts in the P2P broadcaster will cause local resource exhaustion for beacon node operators as an attacker (or reorg/peer-flap conditions) triggers bursts of broadcasts; each call spawns a goroutine that blocks in PublishToTopic until timeout, accumulating many live goroutines.




### Root Cause

 In `beacon-chain/p2p/broadcaster.go` the broadcast entrypoints are non-blocking and spawn a goroutine per call without any fan-in/worker pooling or rate limiting. Each spawned goroutine resets to a fresh background context and applies a long timeout (≈ one epoch) before attempting to publish. When MinimumSyncPeers > 0 and 0 peers are available on the topic, PublishToTopic busy-waits until the context expires, keeping each goroutine alive.

Examples:
- Non-blocking broadcast spawns a goroutine per call:
- internal goroutine uses long timeout and proceeds to publish:
- PublishToTopic waits for peers when MinimumSyncPeers > 0:

### Internal Pre-conditions

 Internal Pre-conditions
*
1. flags.MinimumSyncPeers is greater than 0 (default in realistic node operation).
2. The Service has no peers (or insufficient peers) subscribed to the target gossip topic when the burst occurs.
3. Burst of N broadcast calls occur close in time (e.g., during reorgs, peer flaps, or application-level spikes).
4. params.SecondsPerSlot and params.SlotsPerEpoch imply per-broadcast timeouts long enough to keep goroutines alive (default ≈ epoch duration).
5. Encoding/dialing proceeds (messages are valid enough to encode).

### External Pre-conditions

 1. Network conditions such as reorgs or peer churn reduce effective peers on target topics to 0 temporarily.
2. Attacker or environment can trigger many broadcasts in a short window (e.g., by gossiping events prompting re-broadcast).

### Attack Path

1. The attacker (or adverse network state) causes the node to perform a burst of N broadcasts across one or more subnets.
2. Each broadcast entrypoint immediately spawns a new goroutine (non-blocking) to perform encoding, subnet peer discovery (optional), and publish.
3. With MinimumSyncPeers > 0 and no peers on the topic, each goroutine reaches PublishToTopic and busy-waits for a peer, sleeping in a loop until its context deadline.
4. No peers appear (or not enough in time), so many goroutines remain alive for up to the timeout duration (≈ one epoch), increasing CPU (encoding/dialing overhead), GC and scheduling pressure, and saturating the publish path.

### Impact

The beacon node (affected party) suffers a local denial-of-service risk through resource saturation:
- CPU spikes (encoding SSZ for each message, dialing attempts),
- Increased memory/GC pressure from many concurrent goroutines and buffers,
- Potential publish queue saturation and degraded responsiveness of P2P services,
- Knock-on effects: delayed processing, potential missed duties, slower recovery under churn.
Severity: Medium (Local DoS). Likelihood: Moderate under bursty network events; elevated under adversarial conditions.


Affected Components (representative)
*
- beacon-chain/p2p/broadcaster.go:
  - BroadcastAttestation → go s.internalBroadcastAttestation(...)
  - BroadcastSyncCommitteeMessage → go s.broadcastSyncCommittee(...)
  - BroadcastBlob → go s.internalBroadcastBlob(...)
  - BroadcastDataColumnSidecar → go s.internalBroadcastDataColumnSidecar(...)
- beacon-chain/p2p/pubsub.go:
  - PublishToTopic waits for peers while ctx not done when MinimumSyncPeers > 0



### PoC

```rust
package p2p

import (
	"context"
	"runtime"
	"sync"
	"testing"
	"time"

	p2ptest "github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/beacon-chain/p2p/testing"
	"github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/cmd/beacon-chain/flags"
	"github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/config/params"
	"github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/encoding/bytesutil"
	ethpb "github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/proto/prysm/v1alpha1"
	"github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/time/slots"
	pubsub "github.com/libp2p/go-libp2p-pubsub"
)

// TestService_BroadcastAttestation_GoroutineFanout provides a PoC for Issue F-04 (goroutine fan-out under burst load).
// It triggers a burst of non-blocking BroadcastAttestation calls and asserts that the number of goroutines increases
// notably during the burst window. This uses no console output; only assertions.
func TestService_BroadcastAttestation_GoroutineFanout(t *testing.T) {
	params.SetupTestConfigCleanup(t)
	cfg := params.BeaconConfig().Copy()
	// Keep goroutines alive briefly to observe spike but avoid long runtime.
	cfg.SecondsPerSlot = 1
	cfg.SlotsPerEpoch = 4
	params.OverrideBeaconConfig(cfg)

	// Ensure PublishToTopic waits for peers (do not short-circuit on MinimumSyncPeers == 0).
	resetFlags := flags.Get()
	flags.Init(&flags.GlobalFlags{MinimumSyncPeers: 1})
	defer flags.Init(resetFlags)

	// Single-host setup with pubsub; we intentionally do NOT subscribe any peer to the topic
	// so PublishToTopic waits for peers until context timeout, keeping per-broadcast goroutines alive.
	p := p2ptest.NewTestP2P(t)
	s := &Service{
		host:                  p.BHost,
		pubsub:                p.PubSub(),
		joinedTopics:          map[string]*pubsub.Topic{},
		cfg:                   &Config{},
		genesisTime:           time.Now(),
		genesisValidatorsRoot: bytesutil.PadTo([]byte{'A'}, 32),
		subnetsLock:           make(map[uint64]*sync.RWMutex),
		subnetsLockLock:       sync.Mutex{},
		peers:                 nil, // not needed for this PoC
	}

	// Prepare a fresh attestation at the current slot to avoid being discarded by time checks.
	currSlot := slots.CurrentSlot(s.genesisTime)
	root32 := bytesutil.PadTo([]byte{1}, 32)
	att := &ethpb.Attestation{
		AggregationBits: []byte{0x01},
		Data: &ethpb.AttestationData{
			Slot:            currSlot,
			CommitteeIndex:  0,
			BeaconBlockRoot: root32,
			Source:          &ethpb.Checkpoint{Epoch: 0, Root: root32},
			Target:          &ethpb.Checkpoint{Epoch: 0, Root: root32},
		},
		Signature: bytesutil.PadTo([]byte{2}, 96),
	}
	subnet := uint64(0)

	// Burst settings (kept small so the test runs fast even without Bazel caching).
	burst := 10

	before := runtime.NumGoroutine()
	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
	defer cancel()
	for i := 0; i < burst; i++ {
		// Non-blocking: spawns a goroutine internally
		_ = s.BroadcastAttestation(ctx, subnet, att)
	}
	// Give a brief moment for goroutines to start and block on PublishToTopic.
	time.Sleep(150 * time.Millisecond)
	after := runtime.NumGoroutine()

	// Expect a noticeable increase in goroutine count. Threshold is conservative to avoid flakiness.
	minIncrease := burst / 3 // roughly a third should still be active
	if after-before < minIncrease {
		t.Fatalf("expected at least %d additional goroutines after burst; got delta=%d (before=%d, after=%d)", minIncrease, after-before, before, after)
	}
}

```
How to run (Go):
 go test ./beacon-chain/p2p -run ^TestService_BroadcastAttestation_GoroutineFanout$ -count=1 -timeout=2m



### Mitigation

- Replace direct go-spawn with submission to a bounded work queue per topic.
- Use a small timeout when ListPeers()==0 (e.g., a few hundred ms) and fall back to scheduling a retry rather than blocking for an epoch.
- Guard PublishToTopic with a global in-flight cap; beyond the cap, drop with metrics to preserve node liveness.

title: [INFO] Unnecessary computation in lighthouse implementation of `get_custody_groups`
description:
### Summary

Lighthouse's implementation of `get_custody_groups`, as defined by the `das-core` spec, does not follow the [latest recommendation](https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/blob/master/specs/fulu/das-core.md#get_custody_groups) (main branch) to skip computation if all groups are custodied:

```python
    # Skip computation if all groups are custodied
    if custody_group_count == NUMBER_OF_CUSTODY_GROUPS:
        return [CustodyIndex(i) for i in range(NUMBER_OF_CUSTODY_GROUPS)]

```

Instead, it relies on an [old version of the spec](https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/blob/8e0d0d48e81d6c7c5a8253ab61340f5ea5bac66a/specs/fulu/das-core.md#get_custody_groups), which still loop over all `custody_group_count` to construct the response.

As a result, unnecessary computation is performed.

### Root Cause

Old implementation of the specification

### Internal Pre-conditions

No preconditions

### External Pre-conditions

No preconditions

### Attack Path

No attack. Code optimization.

### Impact

Unnecessary computation on lighthouse clients.

### PoC

See above

### Mitigation

Skip computation if all groups are custodied, as recommended by the latest spec.

title: Dial-before-freshness in subnet broadcasting enables dial exhaustion under stale inputs
`GetInputLengthsMayOverflow` function is redundant and can be removed for code simplification
An attacker can censor broadcasts by ensuring node has exactly one peer on a subnet
description:
### Summary

 A hard-coded broadcast threshold of 1 peer per subnet, combined with a lower-bound check in `hasPeerWithSubnet` that also resolves to 1, will cause the broadcaster to proceed with sending when only a single peer is available. This will cause censorship or DoS for beacon node operators as an attacker can sybil the target subnet so that your node dials exactly one malicious peer; your broadcasts succeed locally but get blackholed.

### Root Cause

 In `broadcaster.go`, `minimumPeersPerSubnetForBroadcast` is hard-coded to 1 and used in the on-demand peer-dial path when no peer is present.
In subnets.go, hasPeerWithSubnet uses the minimum of 1 and the configured MinimumPeersPerSubnet, effectively setting the threshold to 1 for the “ready to broadcast” check.
Result: As soon as there is exactly one peer subscribed to a subnet topic, broadcasting proceeds, even if flags.MinimumPeersPerSubnet is set to a higher value (e.g., 6).


```rust
const minimumPeersPerSubnetForBroadcast = 1

// Non-blocking broadcast ...
if !hasPeer {
    _ = s.FindAndDialPeersWithSubnets(
        ctx, AttestationSubnetTopicFormat, forkDigest,
        minimumPeersPerSubnetForBroadcast, map[uint64]bool{subnet: true},
    )
}
```
```rust
func (s *Service) hasPeerWithSubnet(subnetTopic string) bool {
    minPeers := min(1, flags.Get().MinimumPeersPerSubnet)
    topic := subnetTopic + s.Encoding().ProtocolSuffix()
    peersWithSubnet := s.pubsub.ListPeers(topic)
    return len(peersWithSubnet) >= minPeers
}
```





### Internal Pre-conditions

Broadcaster uses hasPeerWithSubnet to decide if it should publish immediately.
The configured flags.MinimumPeersPerSubnet may be greater than 1, but hasPeerWithSubnet reduces the threshold to 1.
GossipTypeMapping and encoding succeed for the broadcast object.

### External Pre-conditions

 Attacker can supply sybil peers advertising the target subnet(s).
Discovery/dialing selects exactly one such peer for the subnet during the broadcast window.
That peer subscribes to the subnet topic but does not forward (drops messages).

### Attack Path

 Attacker floods discovery with sybil peers on a target subnet.
Your node dials one malicious peer and perceives hasPeerWithSubnet == true (exactly 1 peer).
Your broadcasts succeed locally (no error) and are sent to this single peer.
The malicious peer drops the messages; there is no additional egress path, so messages do not propagate.

### Impact

 Affected party: Beacon node operator and validators using it.
Effects:
Missed attestations; proposer includes fewer or none.
Head lag and chain quality degradation; LC clients may become stale.
Under persistent partition, penalties and MEV opportunity loss.


Affected Components

beacon-chain/p2p/broadcaster.go
BroadcastAttestation/internalBroadcastAttestation
BroadcastBlob/internalBroadcastBlob
BroadcastSyncCommitteeMessage/broadcastSyncCommittee
BroadcastDataColumnSidecar/internalBroadcastDataColumnSidecar
beacon-chain/p2p/subnets.go
hasPeerWithSubnet (threshold collapses to 1)
cmd/beacon-chain/flags/config.go
MinimumPeersPerSubnet is present but bypassed by the min(1, …) check for broadcasting



### PoC


```rust
package p2p

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"
    "reflect"
    "sync"
    "testing"
    "time"

    p2ptest "github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/beacon-chain/p2p/testing"
    "github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/cmd/beacon-chain/flags"
    "github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/config/params"
    "github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/encoding/bytesutil"
    ssz "github.com/prysmaticlabs/fastssz"
    pubsub "github.com/libp2p/go-libp2p-pubsub"
    "github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
    "google.golang.org/protobuf/proto"

    ethpb "github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/proto/prysm/v1alpha1"
    "github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/v6/testing/util"
    "github.com/prysmaticlabs/go-bitfield"
)

// TestService_AttestationBroadcast_SinglePeerThreshold_PoC
// PoC for Issue: Single-peer egress blackholing (censorship via sybil)
// This verifies that the broadcaster considers a single peer on the attestation subnet
// as "enough" to proceed with broadcast, even when the configured MinimumPeersPerSubnet
// is > 1. This aligns with the allegation that if the only peer is malicious (a sink),
// broadcasts succeed locally but may never propagate further.
func TestService_AttestationBroadcast_SinglePeerThreshold_PoC(t *testing.T) {
    // Speed up timeouts for test and ensure deterministic config cleanup.
    params.SetupTestConfigCleanup(t)

    // Configure global flags to require more than 1 peer per subnet.
    prev := flags.Get()
    flags.Init(&flags.GlobalFlags{MinimumPeersPerSubnet: 6})
    t.Cleanup(func() { flags.Init(prev) })

    // Two peers: node under test (p1) and a single sink peer (p2).
    p1 := p2ptest.NewTestP2P(t)
    p2 := p2ptest.NewTestP2P(t)
    p1.Connect(p2)
    require.NotZero(t, len(p1.BHost.Network().Peers()))

    // Service under test backed by p1's host/pubsub.
    s := &Service{
        host:                  p1.BHost,
        pubsub:                p1.PubSub(),
        joinedTopics:          map[string]*pubsub.Topic{},
        cfg:                   &Config{},
        genesisTime:           time.Now(),
        genesisValidatorsRoot: bytesutil.PadTo([]byte{'A'}, 32),
        subnetsLock:           make(map[uint64]*sync.RWMutex),
        subnetsLockLock:       sync.Mutex{},
    }

    // Prepare a valid attestation to avoid encoding/time validation issues.
    att := util.HydrateAttestation(&ethpb.Attestation{AggregationBits: bitfield.NewBitlist(7)})
    // Ensure it implements ssz.Marshaler for broadcastObject encoding path.
    var _ ssz.Marshaler = (*ethpb.Attestation)(nil)

    // Map type to attestation topic and compute concrete topic for subnet 0.
    subnet := uint64(0)
    GossipTypeMapping[reflect.TypeOf(att)] = AttestationSubnetTopicFormat
    digest, err := s.currentForkDigest()
    require.NoError(t, err)
    topic := fmt.Sprintf(AttestationSubnetTopicFormat, digest, subnet)

    // Single sink peer subscribes to the topic (appears as the only subnet peer).
    subWithSuffix := topic + s.Encoding().ProtocolSuffix()
    _, err = p2.SubscribeToTopic(subWithSuffix)
    require.NoError(t, err)

    // Allow libp2p pubsub to process the subscription.
    time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)

    // Assert that hasPeerWithSubnet is true with exactly one peer despite the global
    // MinimumPeersPerSubnet being 6.
    require.True(t, s.hasPeerWithSubnet(topic))
    require.Equal(t, 1, len(s.pubsub.ListPeers(subWithSuffix)))

    // Now broadcast; it should proceed (non-blocking) without attempting to discover
    // additional peers, given hasPeerWithSubnet == true.
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
    defer cancel()
    require.NoError(t, s.BroadcastAttestation(ctx, subnet, att))
}

// TestService_AttestationBroadcast_OnlyOnePeer_NoFanout
// Confirms broadcast succeeds to the only connected peer; this demonstrates that the
// implementation proceeds with a single-peer egress which is susceptible to blackholing
// if that peer is malicious (sink). We assert receive on the single peer using only
// assertions (no prints/logs).
func TestService_AttestationBroadcast_OnlyOnePeer_NoFanout(t *testing.T) {
    params.SetupTestConfigCleanup(t)

    // Require more than 1 peer per subnet in config, but broadcaster will still use 1.
    prev := flags.Get()
    flags.Init(&flags.GlobalFlags{MinimumPeersPerSubnet: 4})
    t.Cleanup(func() { flags.Init(prev) })

    p1 := p2ptest.NewTestP2P(t)
    p2 := p2ptest.NewTestP2P(t)
    p1.Connect(p2)
    require.NotZero(t, len(p1.BHost.Network().Peers()))

    s := &Service{
        host:                  p1.BHost,
        pubsub:                p1.PubSub(),
        joinedTopics:          map[string]*pubsub.Topic{},
        cfg:                   &Config{},
        genesisTime:           time.Now(),
        genesisValidatorsRoot: bytesutil.PadTo([]byte{'A'}, 32),
        subnetsLock:           make(map[uint64]*sync.RWMutex),
        subnetsLockLock:       sync.Mutex{},
    }

    // Fresh valid attestation.
    att := util.HydrateAttestation(&ethpb.Attestation{AggregationBits: bitfield.NewBitlist(7)})
    subnet := uint64(1)

    // Topic mapping and concrete topic for this attestation subnet.
    GossipTypeMapping[reflect.TypeOf(att)] = AttestationSubnetTopicFormat
    digest, err := s.currentForkDigest()
    require.NoError(t, err)
    topic := fmt.Sprintf(AttestationSubnetTopicFormat, digest, subnet)
    topicWithSuffix := topic + s.Encoding().ProtocolSuffix()

    // Sink peer subscribes and awaits exactly one message.
    sub, err := p2.SubscribeToTopic(topicWithSuffix)
    require.NoError(t, err)
    time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)

    // Listen before broadcast to avoid racing.
    recvCtx, recvCancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 1*time.Second)
    defer recvCancel()

    done := make(chan struct{})
    go func() {
        defer close(done)
        msg, e := sub.Next(recvCtx)
        require.NoError(t, e)
        got := &ethpb.Attestation{}
        require.NoError(t, s.Encoding().DecodeGossip(msg.Data, got))
        require.True(t, proto.Equal(got, att))
    }()

    // Broadcast should succeed with only one peer present.
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 1*time.Second)
    defer cancel()
    require.NoError(t, s.BroadcastAttestation(ctx, subnet, att))

    // Ensure the single peer received the message.
    select {
    case <-done:
        // ok
    case <-time.After(2 * time.Second):
        t.Fatal("timed out waiting for single-peer receipt")
    }

    // Verify we still only have one peer in the subnet after broadcast.
    require.Equal(t, 1, len(s.pubsub.ListPeers(topicWithSuffix)))
}

```

Even with MinimumPeersPerSubnet=6, the broadcaster proceeds with exactly 1 peer. If that peer is malicious, messages are blackholed




### Mitigation

 Honor MinimumPeersPerSubnet for the broadcast readiness check.
Replace min(1, flags.MinimumPeersPerSubnet) with flags.MinimumPeersPerSubnet in hasPeerWithSubnet.

title: EstimateGas Not Enforced Against `TX_GAS_LIMIT_CAP` By EIP-7825 In Reth
description:
### Summary

Reth's implementation of `eth_estimateGas` and other simulation pathways overlooks the `EIP-7825` transaction gas cap, which is otherwise enforced during validation. This discrepancy can result in a negative user experience(UX), as gas estimates may exceed the `2^24` cap without any prior notification.


### Root Cause

Code Repository:
- https://github.com/paradigmxyz/reth at `main` branch
Code Commit: 
- https://github.com/paradigmxyz/reth/tree/e8d32a549149b2d1365a20de376300c39c5baa03

Reth enforces the `EIP-7825` tx gas cap in validation, but explicitly disables it for `eth_estimateGas` (and related simulation paths):
https://github.com/paradigmxyz/reth/blob/4e78f956fd5babbaf3c0a9e0b9a9847a6b754dcb/crates/rpc/rpc-eth-api/src/helpers/estimate.rs#L56-L57

```rust
fn estimate_gas_with<S>(
        &self,
        mut evm_env: EvmEnvFor<Self::Evm>,
        mut request: RpcTxReq<<Self::RpcConvert as RpcConvert>::Network>,
        state: S,
        state_override: Option<StateOverride>,
    ) -> Result<U256, Self::Error>
    where
        S: StateProvider,
    {
        …
        …
        // Disable EIP-7825 transaction gas limit to support larger transactions
        evm_env.cfg_env.tx_gas_limit_cap = Some(u64::MAX);

```
The same pattern (disabling cap) is also present in `eth_call/eth_createAccessList` preparation:
```rust
fn create_access_list_with(
        &self,
        mut evm_env: EvmEnvFor<Self::Evm>,
        at: BlockId,
        request: RpcTxReq<<Self::RpcConvert as RpcConvert>::Network>,
        state_override: Option<StateOverride>,
    ) -> impl Future<Output = Result<AccessListResult, Self::Error>> + Send
    where
        Self: Trace,
    {
        …
        …

            // Disable EIP-7825 transaction gas limit to support larger transactions
            evm_env.cfg_env.tx_gas_limit_cap = Some(u64::MAX);
```


Reference: 
- https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-7825.md


### Internal Pre-conditions

The absence of an internal precondition stems from the function's failure to adhere to `EIP-7825`, which caps single transaction gas limit estimations.


### External Pre-conditions

Users can utilize the `eth_estimateGas` API to determine the optimal gas limit for a transaction before it is published on-chain.
```shell
curl -s -X POST localhost:8545 -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  --data '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"eth_estimateGas","params":[{"from":"0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000001","gas":"0xFFFFFFFFFFFF"}]}'
```

### Attack Path

1. Users often use `eth_estimateGas` with a high gas limit when interacting with the Reth chain client.
2. This is done to prevent transaction failures due to insufficient gas.
3. However, if the estimated gas exceeds the 16,777,216 gas cap (as per EIP-7825), the transaction will be rejected during pre-mempool validation.
4. This leads to a poor user experience which violates the efforts of EF to promote the UX of the Ethereum protocols

### Impact

Reth's `eth_estimateGas` function can produce estimates greater than 2^24 (16,777,216). This can lead to a poor user experience because these transactions are later rejected by the transaction pool/consensus validation. This issue is not unique to Reth, as similar problems exist across other Execution Layer (EL) client implementations. This behavior undermines efforts by the Ethereum Foundation to enhance user experience(UX) and prevents early feedback on transaction validity.
While the overall impact is low, enforcing a gas cap would improve the user experience. For reference, see Geth's[`PR-32348`](https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/pull/32348).

### PoC

_No response_

### Mitigation

_No response_
